packTitle: Navigation, Rescue, and Signaling + Water, Food, and Sanitation Import Batch
notes: Normalized from Perplexity category batches for Navigation, Rescue, and Signaling plus Water, Food, and Sanitation.
Land rescue and signaling is treated as the primary context; maritime guides are included as a clearly separate secondary module.
Preparedness guides are mapped to layer 'scenario' to fit the current app layer badge system while retaining guideType='preparedness_guide'.
Use integrationAction to decide whether to upgrade existing broad guides or add new related sub-guides.

categories.summary: Land-first rescue, navigation, and signaling guidance with maritime rescue guides kept as a clearly separate secondary module.

guides.title: Lost on Land: First 30 Minutes
guides.quickAnswer: Stop, calm down, assess your situation, try only a short safe backtrack if the route is obvious, then stay put, shelter, and start signaling.
guides.whenToUse: Use when you realize you may be off-route or lost on land and are not facing an immediate threat like fire, flash flood, or rockfall.
preferredAction: Use STOP: Stop, Think, Observe, Plan.
If you cannot clearly and safely retrace to a known point, stay put and make yourself findable.
backupAction: If communication and safe backtracking are not possible, choose a safer nearby base location, shelter, and begin signaling.
steps: Stop moving as soon as you suspect you are lost.
Control breathing and avoid panic.
Check time, weather, terrain, last known point, and available gear.
If a short, obvious retrace is safe, try it once slowly.
If that fails or is uncertain, stop again and stay put.
Call or text for help if possible and share landmarks or GPS.
Pick a safer base area away from immediate hazards.
Start signaling with whistle, bright items, and light if needed.
Conserve warmth, water, and battery.
warnings: Wandering often moves you farther from the expected search area.
Poor visibility and worsening weather sharply increase travel risk.
Night movement without a clear route is high-risk.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep wandering without a deliberate plan.
Do not split the group.
Do not waste phone battery on non-essential use.
redFlags: You are injured, hypothermic, dehydrated, or trapped by terrain.
Weather is worsening and you lack good shelter.
You are in avalanche terrain, flood drainages, cliff areas, or fire zones and need to move only far enough to reach safer ground.
preparednessTips: Leave a route plan and return time with a trusted contact before trips.
Carry whistle, light, extra warm layer, basic shelter, map, and compass.
Learn local emergency numbers before travel.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor Emergency Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical lost-person guidance for outdoor users.

sourceReferences.title: UIAA Navigation in Poor Visibility
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports conservative movement decisions in poor visibility.

sourceReferences.title: Land SAR stay-vs-go guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful for decision logic and searchability considerations.

guides.title: Stay Put vs Move (Land)
guides.quickAnswer: Default to staying put once you know you are lost, unless your current location is clearly dangerous or you have a short, obvious, safe route to a known point.
guides.whenToUse: Use when you are already lost or uncertain of your location on land and must decide whether to remain or move.
preferredAction: Stay put near your last known point or vehicle once you have called for help or accepted you are lost.
Move only to reach safer shelter or escape immediate danger.
backupAction: If nobody knows your plan, you have no communication, and there is a short, clearly safe daylight route along an obvious feature, you may consider deliberate travel with strict limits.
steps: Ask whether anyone knows your route and return time.
If you are at a vehicle, strongly favor staying with it.
Check for immediate hazards like fire, flood, falling rock, or avalanche terrain.
Compare safe known routes versus unknown terrain and worsening conditions.
If you move, do so only in daylight with a clear turnaround rule.
If you stay, build shelter, concentrate signals, and conserve energy.
warnings: Moving while disoriented can sharply reduce your chances of early rescue.
Fog, storms, darkness, and exhaustion are strong reasons to stop rather than continue traveling.
whatNotToDo: Do not alternate between wandering and staying without a plan.
Do not chase weak phone signal into dangerous terrain.
Do not leave a relatively safe position just because of anxiety alone.
redFlags: You are close to physical limits from cold, heat, dehydration, or injury.
The terrain ahead is steep, dense, unstable, or otherwise hazardous.
Visibility has dropped so much that safe travel is no longer realistic.
preparednessTips: Tell someone your route and turnaround time before going out.
Study major terrain handrails like roads, rivers, and ridgelines before the trip.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor Emergency Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong stay-put guidance tied to expected search areas.

sourceReferences.title: UIAA navigation guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports stopping when visibility and conditions worsen.

sourceReferences.title: Land SAR stay-vs-go guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful for decision framing, with caution on experience-based advice.

guides.title: Whistle Distress Signals (Land)
guides.quickAnswer: Use three loud whistle blasts, repeated regularly with pauses to listen, as your main land distress signal unless a local rescue convention says otherwise.
guides.whenToUse: Use when you are lost or injured on land and need to attract rescuers or nearby people while conserving energy.
preferredAction: Use a whistle from a safe, reasonably open location.
Blow three strong blasts, pause, listen, and repeat regularly.
backupAction: If you know a region-specific convention, such as alpine six-blast signaling, use it only where it clearly applies; otherwise stick to a clear repeated group of three.
steps: Choose a safe location where sound can carry.
Blow three strong blasts in quick succession.
Pause to listen for a response.
Repeat every few minutes rather than continuously.
Pair whistle use with bright visual signals or lights when possible.
warnings: Three-blast patterns are broadly taught, but some alpine regions use six-blast conventions.
Continuous random blowing is less recognizable than distinct patterns.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely on voice shouting alone if a whistle is available.
Do not use random continuous noise instead of a repeated pattern.
Do not ignore region-specific guidance if you know it clearly applies.
redFlags: You are getting dizzy, breathless, or experiencing chest pain while signaling.
You have no response over time but still have usable phone battery or another communication tool.
preparednessTips: Carry a pealess emergency whistle on your body, not buried in a pack.
Teach your group a simple whistle code before trips.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor Emergency Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports whistle signaling for lost outdoor users.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor safety education modules
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Common instruction on distress signal repetition.

sourceReferences.title: Alpine signaling conventions
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful regional context for three vs six signals.

guides.title: Mirror and Light Signaling (Land, Day and Night)
guides.quickAnswer: In daylight, use a signal mirror or reflective surface to flash toward aircraft or distant searchers; at night, flash a bright light in deliberate patterns such as SOS or groups of three.
guides.whenToUse: Use when you can see or hear distant people, vehicles, or aircraft and have sunlight or a usable light source.
preferredAction: Use a proper signal mirror with a sighting hole in direct sun.
At night, use your brightest light in clear repeated patterns instead of constant illumination.
backupAction: If you lack a true signal mirror, use any reflective surface and combine it with passive bright ground signals.
steps: Choose the most visible safe position you can reach.
In daylight, aim repeated mirror flashes toward aircraft or distant search directions.
At night, flash SOS or repeated groups of three with pauses.
Conserve battery by using intermittent patterns instead of leaving lights on constantly.
Combine mirror or light use with whistle and ground symbols.
warnings: Mirrors are ineffective without enough sunlight.
Phone screens are weaker and drain battery quickly compared with dedicated mirrors or flashlights.
Do not blind pilots at close range with a constant beam.
whatNotToDo: Do not use continuous bright light when nobody can see it.
Do not spend all phone battery on screen or light use if it may still be needed for emergency contact.
redFlags: Aircraft or searchers seem nearby but are not noticing you; improve contrast and move to a more open spot if safe.
Your light source is nearly depleted in cold or dangerous conditions.
preparednessTips: Carry a small dedicated signal mirror and a reliable flashlight or headlamp.
Add reflective or high-visibility items to pack or clothing.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor Emergency Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports flashlight and mirror signaling, including SOS use.

sourceReferences.title: Visual distress signal conspicuity testing
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Provides strong evidence that mirrors are highly conspicuous in sun.

sourceReferences.title: Signal mirror usage evaluations
sourceReferences.organization: Survival equipment testing and rescue signaling education
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Practical aiming and usage tips.

guides.title: Ground-to-Air Symbols for Aircraft
guides.quickAnswer: Use large, high-contrast symbols such as V for assistance and X for medical help so aircraft can understand your situation from above.
guides.whenToUse: Use when aircraft search is likely or you can hear or see aircraft overhead and have access to an open area visible from above.
preferredAction: Build one or more standardized symbols using high-contrast materials in as open and flat an area as possible.
backupAction: If you cannot build standard symbols, create a large obvious X, SOS, or directional arrow that clearly marks your position.
steps: Pick the flattest, most open safe area available.
Use bright or strongly contrasting materials like tarps, clothing, branches, or rocks.
Make symbols large enough to be visible from above.
Place yourself near the symbol but do not obstruct it.
Keep the symbol stable unless your situation changes.
warnings: Small or low-contrast symbols may be invisible from search altitude.
These symbols may guide searchers to you but do not guarantee a landing zone.
whatNotToDo: Do not keep changing symbols without reason.
Do not make messy or ambiguous shapes that could look like debris.
redFlags: You have an injured person needing medical help and aircraft are not responding; increase other signals if safe.
Weather has closed in so aircraft are unlikely to fly; shift focus to shelter and ground-based signaling.
preparednessTips: Learn the basic ICAO/NATO meanings for V, X, Y, N, and directional arrows.
Carry a high-visibility tarp or blanket that can help form symbols.

sourceReferences.title: Ground-air visual signal codes
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary standard for V, X, Y, N, and directional symbols.

sourceReferences.title: Air-surface communication training materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful for practical visibility and response context.

guides.title: Beacon and Locator Basics (PLB, EPIRB, Satellite Messengers)
guides.quickAnswer: Use an approved emergency beacon or SOS-capable satellite messenger as your most reliable long-range distress tool, but still pair it with visual and audible signals.
guides.whenToUse: Use for remote travel with limited or no cell service where rescue may be slow or difficult and you need a reliable emergency alerting tool.
preferredAction: For land travel, carry a registered PLB or SOS-capable satellite messenger.
For maritime travel, use a properly registered EPIRB or equivalent marine distress device.
backupAction: If you lack a beacon, rely on voice or text contact if possible, combined with traditional signaling and a clearly left trip plan.
steps: Register and test your beacon according to official guidance before the trip.
In a real emergency, activate SOS and keep the device where it has the clearest possible sky view.
If two-way communication is available, send details about injuries, terrain, and conditions.
Once activated, avoid unnecessary movement unless staying put is clearly unsafe.
Use whistle, light, and visible markers to help rescuers pinpoint you.
warnings: False activations can trigger major rescue responses; use only for real emergencies.
Dense canopy, deep canyons, and buildings can reduce signal quality or delay updates.
whatNotToDo: Do not rely only on phone apps or consumer navigation apps in remote areas.
Do not turn the beacon off once activated unless rescue is complete or you are clearly instructed to do so.
redFlags: Serious injury, severe weather exposure, or terrain trapping with no straightforward self-rescue route.
Loss of safe mobility or survival risk increasing faster than you can self-evacuate.
preparednessTips: Treat a beacon as core safety gear for regular remote trips.
Carry it on your person rather than buried in luggage or pack.
Keep emergency contacts and registration details current.

sourceReferences.title: Beacon registration and use guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary source for registration and owner information requirements.

sourceReferences.title: Marine distress and signaling systems
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful for EPIRB, distress systems, and rescue integration.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency communication planning guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports pairing beacon use with visible rescue signals.

guides.title: Maximizing Rescue Visibility (Land)
guides.quickAnswer: Combine contrast, motion, light, and sound in one place so you stand out from the landscape to both air and ground searchers.
guides.whenToUse: Use when you are waiting for rescue on land after calling for help or activating a beacon, or when you suspect a search is underway.
preferredAction: Create overlapping signals in one location: bright panels, ground symbols, whistle, and mirror or light.
backupAction: If gear is limited, maximize contrast with clothing or natural materials and focus on one clear visible position.
steps: Choose the safest visible location you can reach.
Lay out bright or contrasting items in large blocks or stripes.
Build simple ground symbols and, if useful, an arrow to your exact position.
Use mirror or light for active signaling when searchers may be nearby.
Use whistle patterns regularly and conserve your voice.
If fire signaling is safe and legal, consider it only with strong control and fire safety.
warnings: Signal fires can start wildfires and may be illegal or unsafe depending on conditions.
Hand-waving alone is weak compared with mirrors, lights, and contrast panels.
whatNotToDo: Do not spread signals across multiple locations.
Do not camouflage yourself when you want to be found.
Do not rely on a single method if you have alternatives.
redFlags: Aircraft or vehicles are repeatedly nearby but do not notice you.
Visibility is worsening rapidly from weather, smoke, or darkness.
preparednessTips: Carry at least one high-visibility item and a reflective emergency blanket.
Choose pack and shell colors that are visible if you often travel remotely.

sourceReferences.title: Visual distress signal effectiveness testing
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Controlled visibility testing for mirrors, flares, and hand-waving.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor emergency signaling guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports combined sound and light signaling when lost.

sourceReferences.title: Rescue signaling best practice education
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful for combining multiple methods.

guides.title: Basic Navigation Without GPS (Land)
guides.quickAnswer: Use map and compass if you have them; otherwise use only broad terrain awareness for cautious short decisions, and avoid ambitious travel when lost.
guides.whenToUse: Use when GPS has failed, battery is low, or accuracy is poor and you need a fallback strategy on land.
preferredAction: Use map and compass together to orient yourself and plan short safe movement along obvious terrain handrails like roads, trails, ridges, or major valleys.
backupAction: If you lack tools or skill, use the sun and major terrain only for broad orientation, not precise navigation, and prefer staying put over long uncertain travel.
steps: If you have a map and compass, orient the map to north and identify obvious terrain features around you.
Match roads, rivers, ridges, valleys, and lakes to the map.
Plan short, conservative travel legs along clear handrails.
If visibility is poor or your location estimate is unstable, stop traveling and shelter instead.
If you have no tools, use the sun only for general direction and avoid relying on folk methods as precise navigation.
warnings: Many natural-navigation folklore tricks are unreliable.
Fog, storms, darkness, and whiteout conditions can defeat even experienced navigators.
Streams and valleys do not automatically lead safely to help.
whatNotToDo: Do not over-trust smartphone navigation if battery is low or the device is cold.
Do not commit to long dead-reckoning travel in unfamiliar terrain.
Do not treat moss, plant growth, or similar folklore as reliable directional tools.
redFlags: You repeatedly cannot match terrain to your map or keep changing your estimated position.
Visibility drops to the point that safe travel is no longer realistic.
You are becoming exhausted, hypothermic, or heat-stressed while trying to navigate.
preparednessTips: Carry a paper map and simple baseplate compass even if using GPS.
Practice map and compass skills in easy terrain before relying on them remotely.
Learn local terrain features and hazards before your trip.

sourceReferences.title: Navigation in poor visibility
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong mountain guidance for when to stop or simplify navigation.

sourceReferences.title: Mountain rescue navigation guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports prioritizing map and compass and limiting risky movement.

sourceReferences.title: Outdoor Emergency Plan
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports safe retracing only when obvious and controlled.

guides.title: Maritime Visual Distress Basics (Sea and Large Lakes)
guides.quickAnswer: On water, use coast-guard-approved visual distress signals such as flares, orange distress flags, smoke, and approved electronic lights; treat land-based signaling patterns only as secondary aids.
guides.whenToUse: Use at sea or on large lakes where maritime regulations and search-and-rescue systems apply.
preferredAction: Carry and use approved visual distress devices appropriate to your vessel and waters, supplemented by mirror, horn, and bright visible items.
backupAction: If approved devices are unavailable in a true emergency, use any visible and audible means you have while calling for help by radio or phone.
steps: Know and carry the distress equipment required for your vessel and waters.
In an emergency, use radio first if possible, then deploy the most suitable day or night visual signal.
Use mirrors, bright cloth, and arm motion only as secondary aids.
Continue signaling intermittently rather than wasting all devices at once.
warnings: Pyrotechnic flares and smoke devices can cause burns or fires if handled badly.
Not all bright LED devices meet legal distress-signal standards.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume land whistle or mirror conventions meet maritime legal requirements.
Do not bury distress gear where it cannot be reached quickly.
redFlags: Flooding, fire aboard, loss of propulsion in dangerous conditions, or a serious medical emergency at sea require immediate distress signaling.
If flares or lights get no response, continue with radio and other signaling methods.
preparednessTips: Inspect and replace flares or time-limited devices regularly.
Train all crew on where distress gear is stored and how to deploy it.

sourceReferences.title: Visual Distress Signals
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary source for maritime distress-signal requirements and legal distinctions.

sourceReferences.title: Visual distress signal effectiveness testing
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Evidence for signal visibility in real-world conditions.

guides.title: Maritime Electronic Distress Basics (Sea and Large Lakes)
guides.quickAnswer: At sea, use VHF radio distress calling, DSC, EPIRBs, and other approved electronic systems as primary alerting tools, with visual signals as backup for localization.
guides.whenToUse: Use for boating or offshore travel where marine radio and beacon systems are part of the emergency response system.
preferredAction: Use VHF marine radio, DSC, and EPIRBs or similar approved devices as your main distress systems.
backupAction: If marine systems are unavailable, use any phone, satellite device, or general PLB while staying with the vessel if safe.
steps: Program and test marine radio and distress features before the trip.
Register and maintain EPIRBs correctly.
In distress, send a DSC alert if available, make a Mayday call, and activate the EPIRB if needed.
Maintain watch for responses and continue visual signaling as rescuers approach.
warnings: Marine systems depend on correct setup, registration, and user familiarity.
Loss of vessel power can disable radios; self-powered beacons are a critical backup.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume a phone alone is enough offshore.
Do not turn off an activated beacon without clear rescue-resolution reason or instructions.
redFlags: Any major loss of vessel safety or serious medical emergency at sea warrants immediate distress communication.
preparednessTips: Practice distress procedures with crew.
Store handheld radios and beacons where they can be reached instantly in abandon-ship situations.

sourceReferences.title: Marine distress communications overview
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary source for Mayday, distress systems, and safety communication.

sourceReferences.title: Integrated maritime distress systems
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports role of DSC, EPIRB, and related systems.

categories.summary: Emergency drinking water, outage food safety, and sanitation guidance, with purification and contamination limits separated clearly from non-drinking sanitation tasks.

guides.title: Choosing Safe Hydration Sources
guides.quickAnswer: Use sealed bottled water first; use tap water only if officials say it is safe; otherwise boil or disinfect the clearest available source before drinking.
guides.whenToUse: Use when an emergency disrupts normal water service or you are unsure which available water is safest to drink, cook with, or use for brushing teeth.
preferredAction: Drink sealed bottled water from trusted sources first.
Use utility tap water only when authorities explicitly say it is safe.
backupAction: If neither is available, use the clearest water from the safest protected source you can find and then boil or disinfect it before use.
steps: Check local emergency, health, or water authority alerts.
Rank your sources from safest to riskiest.
Reserve the safest water for drinking, infant formula, food preparation, brushing teeth, and wound cleaning.
Use lower-quality water only for non-contact uses like flushing or cleaning.
warnings: Boiling or bleach does not remove fuel, toxic chemicals, or heavy metals.
Floodwater is usually contaminated with sewage, chemicals, and debris and should be treated as unsafe for drinking.
whatNotToDo: Do not assume clear-looking water is safe.
Do not drink from radiators, boilers, chemically treated pools, or hot tubs.
Do not ignore boil-water or do-not-drink notices.
redFlags: Vomiting, diarrhea, or fever after drinking questionable water.
Known sewage or chemical contamination exposure.
Infants, older adults, pregnant people, or immunocompromised people showing dehydration signs.
preparednessTips: Store at least 1 gallon per person per day for at least 3 days, with 2 weeks as a better target if possible.
Keep printed boiling and bleach-treatment instructions with your emergency kit.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency water safety guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong public guidance on choosing safe emergency water sources.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency disinfection of drinking water
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary source for treatment methods and limitations.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency WASH materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports practical emergency communication and contamination limits.

guides.title: Purifying Water by Boiling
guides.quickAnswer: Bring clear water to a rolling boil for at least 1 minute, or 3 minutes at high altitude, then cool and store it covered.
guides.whenToUse: Use when bottled water is unavailable and you must drink questionable tap, well, or surface water because of an emergency or boil-water advisory.
preferredAction: Use boiling as the primary household method for making microbiologically unsafe water safer to drink whenever you have a safe heat source.
backupAction: If boiling is impossible, use unscented household bleach or approved purification tablets according to instructions.
steps: If water is cloudy, let particles settle and pre-filter through a clean cloth or filter.
Heat the water until it reaches a rolling boil.
Boil for at least 1 minute, or 3 minutes above about 2,000 meters elevation.
Allow it to cool naturally.
Store it in a clean covered container and label it as boiled drinking water.
warnings: Boiling does not remove chemical contamination like fuel, pesticides, or heavy metals.
Hot water handling can cause serious burns.
whatNotToDo: Do not boil water in unsafe containers that may melt or leach chemicals.
Do not add unsafe ice to cooled boiled water.
Do not mix boiled water back into untreated water.
redFlags: Waterborne illness symptoms after drinking boiled water may point to non-microbial contamination or another unsafe source.
If authorities issue a do-not-drink advisory for chemicals, do not rely on boiling.
preparednessTips: Store extra fuel and safe cooking equipment as part of emergency planning.
Keep heat-safe pots reserved for emergency water boiling.
Practice boiling and storing water safely before emergencies.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency disinfection of drinking water
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary boiling guidance and emergency treatment standards.

sourceReferences.title: How to make water safe in an emergency
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Confirms boiling as a preferred household emergency method.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency drinking water guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports boiling time and contamination-limit framing.

guides.title: Purifying Water with Household Liquid Bleach
guides.quickAnswer: Use plain unscented household bleach of known strength to disinfect relatively clear water, then wait at least 30 minutes before drinking.
guides.whenToUse: Use when bottled or boiled water is unavailable and you must disinfect relatively clear water from a utility, well, or surface source.
preferredAction: Use regular unscented liquid bleach with known sodium hypochlorite strength and follow emergency dosing guidance.
backupAction: If bleach is unavailable, use approved purification tablets or boil water when possible.
steps: Confirm the bleach is plain, unscented, and not splashless or color-safe.
If water is cloudy, let it settle and pre-filter it.
Add the correct emergency dose for the bleach strength and water volume.
Stir or shake and let it stand at least 30 minutes.
If there is no slight chlorine smell afterward, dose once more and wait again.
Store treated water in clean covered containers.
warnings: Bleach is less effective in very cloudy or very cold water.
Bleach does not make chemically contaminated water safe.
whatNotToDo: Do not use scented, splashless, color-safe, or additive-containing bleach.
Do not guess bleach strength if the label is missing.
Do not mix bleach with other cleaners or chemicals.
Do not drink concentrated bleach or overly strong solutions.
redFlags: Burning, breathing problems, or severe pain after concentrated bleach exposure.
Persistent vomiting, stomach pain, or diarrhea after treated water.
preparednessTips: Keep a small bottle of plain unscented bleach in your emergency kit and replace it periodically.
Store printed dosage tables for common container sizes and bleach strengths.
Train adult household members on correct dosing.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency disinfection of drinking water
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary source for bleach disinfection method and limits.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency water safety guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports emergency chlorination use and public messaging.

sourceReferences.title: Local emergency chlorination adaptations
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful for household dosing context.

guides.title: When Treated Water Is Still Unsafe to Drink
guides.quickAnswer: Water can remain unsafe after boiling or chlorination if it contains fuel, toxic chemicals, or heavy metals, or if authorities issue a do-not-drink notice.
guides.whenToUse: Use when you have already boiled or disinfected water but suspect chemical contamination or are under a stronger official advisory than a normal boil-water notice.
preferredAction: Obey do-not-drink or do-not-use advisories and use only confirmed safe water for drinking, cooking, brushing teeth, and infant formula.
backupAction: If no safe drinking water is available, use suspect water only for limited non-drinking tasks like flushing if allowed and seek emergency water distribution quickly.
steps: Check whether the advisory is boil-water, do-not-drink, or do-not-use.
Inspect the water for oily sheen, strong chemical or fuel odor, or unusual contamination indicators.
Use safe water only for high-risk activities like drinking, cooking, infant feeding, brushing teeth, dish rinsing, and wound cleaning.
Reserve suspect water only for low-risk non-contact uses if officials allow it.
warnings: Floodwater is often contaminated with sewage and chemicals.
Some dangerous contaminants have no obvious taste or smell.
whatNotToDo: Do not drink water from vehicles, heating systems, or radiators.
Do not wash open wounds or medical devices with chemically suspect water.
Do not assume boiling fixes chemical hazards.
redFlags: Sudden dizziness, chest pain, breathing problems, or confusion after exposure to contaminated water.
Known industrial or fuel contamination in the water system.
preparednessTips: Store extra safe water so you are less likely to drink questionable sources.
Know where local emergency water distribution points may be located.
Keep a battery or solar radio for advisories.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency water safety and treatment limits
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Clear distinction between boil-water and do-not-drink situations.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency disinfection of drinking water
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Important limits of boiling and chlorination methods.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency WASH materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports practical messaging on contamination and use restrictions.

guides.title: Emergency Water Storage at Home
guides.quickAnswer: Store at least 1 gallon of safe water per person per day for at least 3 days, and ideally up to 2 weeks, in clean food-grade containers.
guides.whenToUse: Use before emergencies, or when you still have safe water and time to prepare for outages, storms, or disaster disruption.
preferredAction: Store safe tap or bottled water in clean tightly sealed food-grade containers in cool, dark locations away from chemicals.
backupAction: If ideal containers are unavailable, carefully reuse suitable beverage bottles such as PET soda bottles after proper cleaning and sanitizing.
steps: Calculate at least 1 gallon per person per day, with more for heat, pets, illness, or special needs.
Choose food-grade containers that close tightly and have not stored chemicals.
Wash, rinse, sanitize, and dry self-filled containers before use.
Fill with safe water and label with date and drinking-water designation.
Store away from sunlight, heat, fuels, pesticides, and chemical vapors.
Rotate self-filled stored water every 6 months.
warnings: Vapors from gasoline, pesticides, and chemicals can penetrate some plastics.
Milk jugs and some food containers are poor long-term choices because residues can promote bacterial growth.
whatNotToDo: Do not partially close containers.
Do not keep topping off old water without emptying, cleaning, and refilling.
Do not assume unknown plastics are food-grade.
redFlags: Stored water smells foul, appears slimy, or has visible growth.
An emergency occurs and you have no safe stored water and unsafe public supply.
preparednessTips: Aim for a 2-week supply if space and budget allow.
Store smaller grab-and-go bottles as well as larger home containers.
Use reminders to rotate stored water every 6 months.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency water supply guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary household storage quantity and handling guidance.

sourceReferences.title: Household water storage best practices
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports container cleaning, storage, and rotation.

guides.title: Using Non-Drinking Water Safely
guides.quickAnswer: Reserve suspect or lower-quality water for low-risk tasks like flushing or cleaning non-food-contact surfaces, and keep it away from drinking, cooking, wounds, and infant feeding.
guides.whenToUse: Use when you have lower-quality or questionable water that is not safe to drink but may still be useful for limited non-drinking household needs.
preferredAction: Clearly separate safe drinking water from non-drinking water and use only the lowest-risk source for each task.
backupAction: If quality is uncertain and official guidance is unavailable, use questionable water only for flushing or other minimal-contact tasks.
steps: Label containers clearly as drinking or non-drinking water.
Use safe water for food, teeth, dishes that contact food, infant feeding, and wounds.
Use lower-quality water only for flushing, some cleaning tasks, or other non-contact uses if allowed.
Avoid splashing contaminated water into living or food-prep areas.
Discard non-drinking water if it shows obvious chemical contamination that makes even handling unsafe.
warnings: Using unsafe water for produce, utensils, wounds, or infant feeding can cause illness.
Gray water is not safe for handwashing or food contact.
whatNotToDo: Do not rinse ready-to-eat foods with unsafe water.
Do not wash open wounds with suspect water.
Do not use contaminated water for final dish rinses or handwashing before food prep.
redFlags: Chemical odor, oily sheen, or irritation from skin contact with suspect water.
Household illness cluster after using questionable water in food or cleaning processes.
preparednessTips: Store extra water specifically designated for hygiene and cleaning.
Keep written instructions on which tasks require fully safe water.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency water safety guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports safe vs unsafe uses of compromised water.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency WASH materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Useful for separating drinking and non-drinking water tasks.

guides.title: Refrigerator Safety During Outages
guides.quickAnswer: Keep the fridge closed; perishable foods are generally safe for about 4 hours without power if the door stays shut and temperatures stay cold enough.
guides.whenToUse: Use during and immediately after a power outage when you still have refrigerated food that may be at risk.
preferredAction: Keep refrigerator doors closed as much as possible.
Use an appliance thermometer to confirm temperature stays at or below 4°C / 40°F.
backupAction: If the outage will exceed about 4 hours, move the most perishable items to a cooler with ice or gel packs if you can keep them cold enough.
steps: Before outages, keep a thermometer in the refrigerator.
During outage, keep the door closed.
After outage, check temperature.
Discard high-risk foods that were above 4°C / 40°F for too long.
Keep low-risk items only if still cold and otherwise safe.
warnings: Danger-zone temperatures allow rapid bacterial growth.
Smell and appearance are not reliable safety tests.
whatNotToDo: Do not repeatedly open the refrigerator just to check.
Do not taste food to test safety.
Do not keep leftovers that sat warm too long.
redFlags: Several people get vomiting, diarrhea, or fever after eating outage-affected refrigerated food.
Vulnerable household members have symptoms after eating questionable food.
preparednessTips: Keep a thermometer in the fridge.
Store shelf-stable foods that do not require refrigeration.
Plan coolers and ice packs for longer outages.

sourceReferences.title: Food safety during power outages
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary rules for refrigerator safety and discard decisions.

guides.title: Freezer Safety During Outages
guides.quickAnswer: A full freezer can stay cold about 48 hours, or 24 hours if half-full, if the door stays closed; food with ice crystals may still be safe to refreeze or cook.
guides.whenToUse: Use during and after a power outage when frozen food may be thawing.
preferredAction: Keep the freezer closed and monitor with a thermometer if possible.
backupAction: If the outage will be long, group foods together and move critical items to coolers with ice or dry ice if available.
steps: Before outages, keep the freezer cold and reasonably full.
During outage, keep the door closed.
After outage, check for ice crystals and safe temperature.
Discard foods that have been above safe temperatures too long.
Refreeze only foods that stayed cold enough, understanding quality may decline.
warnings: Food can become unsafe even if it still looks normal.
Do not rely only on smell or color.
whatNotToDo: Do not open the freezer repeatedly.
Do not refreeze food that stayed warm too long.
Do not leave thawed food at room temperature for hours.
redFlags: Household members become sick after eating outage-affected previously frozen food.
preparednessTips: Keep a freezer thermometer installed.
Store extra ice or gel packs for coolers.
Label and group foods for quicker decision-making.

sourceReferences.title: Food safety during power outages
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary source for freezer hold times and safety decisions.

guides.title: Deciding When to Throw Away Food After an Outage
guides.quickAnswer: When in doubt, throw it out—discard perishable foods that were too warm too long, and never taste food to decide if it is safe.
guides.whenToUse: Use when power returns and you must decide what food to keep or discard from a refrigerator or freezer.
preferredAction: Use time-and-temperature rules and err on the side of discarding questionable foods.
backupAction: If you lack thermometers, use outage duration, door opening frequency, and remaining ice crystals to make conservative discard decisions.
steps: Identify high-risk foods like meat, fish, poultry, eggs, dairy, and leftovers.
Apply refrigerator and freezer temperature rules.
Keep lower-risk foods only when clearly safe.
Discard swollen, leaking, badly damaged, or obviously spoiled items.
Avoid feeding questionable food to high-risk people.
warnings: Tasting food is not a safe test.
Some dangerous bacteria do not change smell or appearance.
whatNotToDo: Do not refreeze or keep food that warmed too long.
Do not feed suspect foods to children, pregnant people, older adults, or immunocompromised people.
redFlags: Multiple people become ill after eating the same questionable food.
Neurological symptoms after canned food suggest a possible botulism emergency.
preparednessTips: Keep printed food keep/discard lists from FoodSafety.gov.
Use fridge and freezer thermometers routinely.
Store shelf-stable alternatives for outages.

sourceReferences.title: Food safety in emergencies and power outages
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Primary guidance on what to keep or discard after outages.

guides.title: Safe Food Handling When Water Is Limited
guides.quickAnswer: Use safe water for any food-contact task, and if safe water is scarce, prioritize drinking, medication, hand hygiene before food prep, and rinsing food-contact items.
guides.whenToUse: Use during emergencies when safe water is limited but you still need to prepare food or clean utensils and surfaces.
preferredAction: Use safe water for handwashing before food prep, washing produce that will be eaten raw, rinsing utensils, and cleaning food-contact surfaces.
backupAction: If safe water is scarce, use alcohol-based hand sanitizer on visibly clean hands and prioritize disposable utensils and minimal-contact food prep.
steps: Prioritize safe water for drinking, medications, hand hygiene, food rinsing, and dish rinsing.
Wash hands with safe water and soap when possible.
Use hand sanitizer on visibly clean hands when water is limited.
Use safe water for produce eaten raw.
If dishwashing water is limited, scrape first, wash in small basins, and sanitize with safe-water-based solution.
warnings: Using contaminated water on produce, utensils, or surfaces can re-contaminate food.
Gray water is not safe for food handling or handwashing.
whatNotToDo: Do not rinse ready-to-eat foods with unsafe water.
Do not reuse dirty dishwater for final rinsing or hand hygiene.
redFlags: Cluster of gastrointestinal illness after changes in water use or dishwashing.
Vomiting or diarrhea in vulnerable household members.
preparednessTips: Store extra safe water labeled for cooking and kitchen hygiene.
Keep disposable utensils and sanitizer in emergency kits.
Plan low-water food preparation methods in advance.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency WASH and food safety guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports safe kitchen use of limited water.

guides.title: Emergency Toilet and Waste Management
guides.quickAnswer: Keep human waste contained, covered, and away from food, living spaces, and water sources using lined buckets, improvised toilets, or approved disposal methods.
guides.whenToUse: Use when toilets do not flush or sewage systems are damaged, unavailable, or backing up during an emergency.
preferredAction: Use functioning toilets only while authorities say sewer systems are safe.
If not, set up an improvised toilet that keeps waste sealed and separated from people, food, and water.
backupAction: If no toilet setup is possible, use a designated emergency latrine or pit only where local conditions and guidance make that safe.
steps: Stop flushing if sewage systems are not working and backup is possible.
Set up a sturdy bucket or toilet frame lined with heavy-duty bags.
Add absorbent material after each use.
Seal and store bags in a covered container until safe disposal.
Keep waste areas away from food, children, and pets.
Follow local disposal instructions if available.
warnings: Improper waste disposal can spread diarrheal disease and contaminate water sources.
Do not dispose of waste into open water or floodwater.
whatNotToDo: Do not mix emergency toilet waste with regular household trash unless authorities instruct it.
Do not reuse waste bags or containers without thorough disinfection.
Do not allow sewage backup to continue unchecked if you can safely isolate the problem.
redFlags: Community outbreak-like diarrhea after sanitation disruption.
Visible sewage backing into homes or communal living areas.
Leaking or unsealed waste storage near living spaces.
preparednessTips: Store heavy-duty bags, absorbent material, disinfectants, and a bucket or portable toilet.
Learn local emergency waste disposal guidance in advance.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency WASH sanitation guidance
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Strong basis for emergency toilet setup and contamination prevention.

guides.title: Hand Hygiene When Water or Soap Is Scarce
guides.quickAnswer: Wash hands with safe water and soap when possible; otherwise use alcohol-based sanitizer on visibly clean hands and prioritize hygiene before eating, feeding children, or wound care.
guides.whenToUse: Use in emergencies with limited safe water, damaged water systems, or crowded conditions that increase disease spread.
preferredAction: Wash with safe water and soap for at least 20 seconds after toilet use and before food or wound care.
backupAction: If safe water or soap is unavailable, use alcohol-based sanitizer on visibly clean hands after wiping away dirt if possible.
steps: Clean hands after toilet use, waste handling, food prep, feeding, and wound care.
Use safe water and soap when available.
If using sanitizer, cover all hand surfaces and rub until dry.
Set up hygiene stations near toilets and food prep if possible.
warnings: Sanitizer works poorly on visibly dirty or greasy hands.
Crowded shelters and disrupted sanitation increase disease spread.
whatNotToDo: Do not wash hands before food prep with contaminated water.
Do not wipe hands on visibly dirty cloths as a substitute for hand cleaning.
redFlags: Rising diarrhea, vomiting, or respiratory illness despite hygiene efforts in a household or shelter.
preparednessTips: Include soap, sanitizer, and disposable towels in emergency kits.
Plan dedicated handwashing or hygiene stations near toilet and kitchen areas.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency hygiene guidance
sourceReferences.organization: CDC WASH and IFRC / Red Cross
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports hand hygiene priorities under limited-water conditions.

guides.title: Cleaning and Disinfection to Prevent Disease Spread
guides.quickAnswer: Clean visible dirt first, then disinfect high-touch and contaminated surfaces with an appropriate disinfectant or bleach solution made using safe water.
guides.whenToUse: Use during and after emergencies when flooding, sewage issues, or illness increase contamination risk around the home or shelter.
preferredAction: Use safe water, soap or detergent, and an appropriate disinfectant on toilets, kitchens, handles, and visibly contaminated areas.
backupAction: If supplies are limited, prioritize toilets, food-prep areas, and high-touch surfaces first.
steps: Remove visible dirt with soap and water before disinfecting.
Prepare disinfectant safely according to instructions.
Disinfect toilets, sinks, counters, handles, and contaminated surfaces.
Allow the proper contact time before drying or wiping.
Use gloves and ventilation when using bleach or disinfectants.
warnings: Floodwater or sewage-contaminated items may need to be discarded if they cannot be cleaned effectively.
Mixing bleach with ammonia or acids creates toxic fumes.
whatNotToDo: Do not use contaminated water for cleaning food-contact surfaces.
Do not mix cleaning products together.
Do not use the same tools for toilet and kitchen cleaning without proper disinfection.
redFlags: Rashes, breathing issues, or eye irritation after chemical exposure.
Persistent sewage contamination after cleaning attempts.
preparednessTips: Store detergent, disinfectant, bleach, gloves, buckets, and cloths.
Keep printed dilution and safety instructions with your supplies.
Use separate cleaning tools for toilets versus kitchens.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency environmental cleaning guidance
sourceReferences.organization: CDC WASH and emergency hygiene materials
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Supports household cleaning and disinfection after contamination events.
